title: Stilton and walnut scones
subtitle: Savoury blue cheese scones with toasted walnuts
excerpt: Savoury scones built around Stilton and chopped walnuts. No sugar. The blue cheese melts through the crumb as the scones bake, leaving pockets of sharp, salty flavour. Serve warm with butter, or alongside a cheese board instead of crackers.
sourceNotes: Savoury cheese scones are a well-established British baking tradition, most commonly made with Cheddar. The Stilton and walnut combination follows the same structure, substituting blue cheese for the standard hard cheese. The flavour pairing of Stilton with walnuts is a long-standing British combination, most often found on cheeseboard selections. This recipe applies that pairing to the savoury scone format.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze once baked and cooled. Reheat from frozen at 180 degrees C fan for 10 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the day they are made. Good served warm with a bowl of soup.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; high heat gives a good rise and lightly coloured top

glossaryTerms.definition: Stilton crumbled into scone dough does not distribute evenly the way grated Cheddar does. Break it into small irregular pieces and scatter through the dough rather than stirring it in; stirring smears the cheese. Some pieces will melt into the crumb; others will stay as distinct pockets of blue cheese. Both are desirable. Do not add extra salt to the dough: Stilton carries enough.

content.text: These scones contain no sugar. The fat content from the Stilton keeps the crumb tender in the same way butter does. Understanding how
tells you why crumbling rather than grating is the right approach here. Do not overwork the dough after adding the cheese.

Heat the oven to 220 degrees C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Combine 250 g flour, 2 tsp baking powder, and 0.25 tsp salt in a bowl. Rub in 50 g cold butter to rough breadcrumbs. Scatter in 100 g crumbled Stilton and 60 g chopped walnuts and mix briefly to distribute. Beat the egg with the buttermilk. Add most of the liquid and mix with a table knife until the dough just comes together. It will be soft and slightly shaggy.

Turn out onto a lightly floured surface and pat to 3 cm thick. Cut into rounds with a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down. Do not twist. Place on the lined tray. Brush the tops with reserved beaten egg. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until golden and risen. Cool on a wire rack for a few minutes before serving warm.

items.solution: The Stilton was particularly well-aged or already well-salted. Taste your Stilton before baking and reduce or omit the added salt accordingly.

items.problem: Scones spread sideways rather than rising up
items.solution: The dough was too wet, or the butter was not cold enough. The dough should be just barely holding together. Pat to 3 cm thick and do not handle more than necessary.
